title: CO₂ emissions per capita vs. fossil fuel consumption per capita
subtitle: Fossil fuel consumption is measured as the average consumption of energy from coal, oil and gas per person. [Fossil fuel and industry emissions](#dod:fossilemissions) are included. [Land-use change emissions](#dod:land-use-change-emissions) are not included.
caption:
CO₂ emissions per capita vs. fossil fuel consumption per capita

Fossil fuel consumption is measured as the average consumption of energy from coal, oil and gas per person. Fossil fuel and industry emissions are included. Land-use change emissions are not included.

This scatter plot displays the relationship between per capita CO₂ emissions and per capita energy consumption from fossil fuels for the year 2024. The vertical axis represents CO₂ emissions in tonnes per person, while the horizontal axis measures energy from fossil fuels in kilowatt-hours per person; both axes utilize a logarithmic scale. Data points are color-coded by income group, with pink indicating lower-middle-income countries, light green for upper-middle-income countries, and dark green for high-income countries. The size of each circle corresponds to the country's population. A positive correlation is visible across the chart, where higher energy consumption generally aligns with higher emissions. Qatar sits at the top right, showing the highest values on both axes, exceeding 20 tonnes of emissions and 100,000 kWh of energy. Other high-income nations like Kuwait, Saudi Arabia, the United States, and Singapore cluster in the upper-right quadrant with emissions ranging roughly between 10 and 20 tonnes. China and Japan appear as large circles in the middle of the distribution, with emissions near 10 tonnes and energy consumption between 20,000 and 50,000 kWh. European countries such as the United Kingdom, Germany, and France group together with emissions between 5 and 10 tonnes. In the lower-left section, countries like India, Indonesia, and the Philippines show lower values, with emissions generally below 3 tonnes and energy consumption under 10,000 kWh. Pakistan is positioned at the bottom left with the lowest visible values on the chart.
